sections.java: // java.time — immutable, fluent import java.time.*; LocalDate today = LocalDate.now(); LocalDate later = today.plusDays(30); Instant nowUtc = Instant.now(); Duration d = Duration.ofHours(5);
sections.python: # datetime — also immutable from datetime import datetime, date, timedelta, timezone today = date.today() later = today + timedelta(days=30) # + operator! now_utc = datetime.now(timezone.utc) d = timedelta(hours=5)

sections.title: timedelta Does Arithmetic With Operators
sections.content: Where Java uses plusDays/minus, Python overloads + and -: deadline = now + timedelta(days=7), and subtracting two datetimes yields a timedelta you can read as .days / .total_seconds(). Parse ISO 8601 with datetime.fromisoformat(s) and emit it with .isoformat() — the analog of DateTimeFormatter.ISO_INSTANT.

sections.title: datetime.now() Is Naive — and That Bites
sections.content: datetime.now() returns a naive datetime with no timezone, unlike Java's Instant which is always UTC. Naive and aware datetimes can't be compared or subtracted, and naive ones silently assume "local time." Always make server/data timestamps aware: datetime.now(timezone.utc). For named zones use zoneinfo.ZoneInfo("America/New_York") (the ZoneId analog, stdlib since 3.9).

sections.java: // Parse & format with a formatter DateTimeFormatter fmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd"); LocalDate d = LocalDate.parse("2026-06-24", fmt); String s = d.format(fmt);
sections.python: # strptime parses, strftime formats d = datetime.strptime("2026-06-24", "%Y-%m-%d") s = d.strftime("%Y-%m-%d") # For ISO 8601, skip the pattern entirely: d = datetime.fromisoformat("2026-06-24")

sections.question: Why prefer datetime.now(timezone.utc) over datetime.now()?
options: now() returns a naive datetime (no tz); the aware UTC form avoids comparison errors and ambiguous local-time bugs
Only the utc form can be formatted
sections.explanation: datetime.now() is naive — it carries no timezone, unlike Java's always-UTC Instant. Naive and timezone-aware datetimes can't be compared or subtracted, and naive values silently assume local time. Using datetime.now(timezone.utc) gives an unambiguous, aware timestamp.
